mappings.rationale: Directly covers blood as the circulating fluid (B.2), including haemoglobin and oxygen transport, plasma, red cells, blood pressure, and transfusion.
Covers physiological mechanisms of human respiration (A.7), cardiovascular circulation (B.3.f), and renal excretion (C.2.c) as integrated systems.
Addresses anatomical structures of the heart, lymphatic system, and central nervous system relevant to respiratory organs (A.3.b), circulatory anatomy (B.3), and lymph (B.2.d).
Covers respiratory, circulatory, and excretory organ diversity across invertebrate phyla (A.3.a, B.3.a, C.2.b) and vertebrates.
Addresses lymphocytes and lymph in vertebrates (B.2.d) and the role of white blood cells as formed elements of circulating fluid (B.2.c).
Covers plant internal transport (B.4) including xylem and phloem vascular systems in seed plants and other tracheophytes.
Provides the physics of fluid flow and hydrodynamics underlying circulatory biodynamics (B.3.e) and respiratory gas dynamics (A.4).
Covers hormonal regulation of blood pressure, water balance, and urine production relevant to circulatory control and renal excretion (C.2).
Covers adaptations for aquatic gas exchange and respiratory problems faced by aquatic animals (A.2, A.6).
Covers amphibian respiratory adaptations bridging aquatic gills and terrestrial lungs (A.3.b, A.6), illustrating the evolution of vertebrate gas exchange.
Addresses embryonic development of the circulatory system (B.3.d) including formation of the heart, blood vessels, and excretory organs.
